one_line_takeaway: By combining an Agentless-style deterministic workflow with two purpose-built LLM agents -- one for adaptive repository context collection, one for safety-property-based semantic root-cause analysis via PoC-validated instrumentation -- VulnResolver lifts real-world C/C++ vulnerability-issue resolution on SEC-bench Lite from a 48.8% base-workflow rate to 75.0%, beating the strongest prior baseline (PatchAgent) by 30.4% relative at a lower per-issue LLM cost.
key_finding: On SEC-bench Lite, VulnResolver (DeepSeek-V3.2-Exp) resolves 60/80 issues (75.0%), versus the best SWE-system baseline SWE-agent+DeepSeek-V3.2-Exp at 30/80 (37.5%) and the best prior AVR baseline PatchAgent+DeepSeek-V3.2-Exp or PatchAgent+o3-mini at 46/80 (57.5%) -- a 30.4% relative improvement over the strongest baseline. VulnResolver also wins on 12 of 13 per-project breakdowns in Table 4, ranking second only on njs. Cost is $0.072/issue (10 issues resolved per dollar), cheaper than SWE-agent ($0.108/issue under the same model) despite higher accuracy. The ablation study (Table 5) shows: base Agentless-style workflow alone (VulnResolverbase) already resolves 48.8% at $0.0296/issue (beating SWE-agent's 37.5%); adding only CPCAgent raises this to 61.3% ($0.0431); adding only SPAAgent raises it to 68.8% ($0.0640) -- SPAAgent's standalone contribution exceeds CPCAgent's; combining both (VulnResolverfull) reaches 75.0% ($0.0718), a 53.8% relative improvement over the base workflow. Replacing PoC-based patch selection with simple majority voting drops performance to 62.5% (-16.7% relative). Replacing issue reports with sanitizer logs as input drops performance to 63.8% (-15.0% relative), supporting the paper's central claim that natural-language issue reports carry richer semantic signal than raw sanitizer output. Patch-space-size sweep (T=1..10) shows T=1 already resolves 49/80 (61.3%), T=5 resolves 60/80 at $0.0718, and gains plateau beyond T=5 (61/80 at T=10, $0.0768). Base-model ablation (Table 7) confirms VulnResolverfull consistently beats VulnResolverbase across GPT-4o (+51.9% relative), o3-mini (+64.3%), and DeepSeek-V3.2-Exp (+53.8%). On SEC-bench Full (RQ3, 200 issues), VulnResolver fixes 135/200 (67.5%), beating the SEC-bench public leaderboard's best system (OpenHands + Claude 3.7 Sonnet, 34.0%) by 98.5% relative, and beating its own VulnResolverbase by 37.8% relative, with best-in-project results on 23/29 projects. Average tool usage: ~34 calls/issue; CPCAgent averages 5.7 code searches, 2.9 reads, 2.4 symbol resolutions per issue; SPAAgent averages 6.2 PoC executions, 6.3 edits applied, 0.96 Python executions per issue.
core_contribution: VulnResolver, an LLM-based 'hybrid agent' framework for automated vulnerability issue resolution (AVR) in real-world C/C++ codebases. It layers two specialized LLM agents on top of an Agentless-style deterministic workflow: (1) the Context Pre-Collection Agent (CPCAgent), which uses static-analysis tools (code search, LSP-based symbol resolution via clangd) to adaptively explore the target repository and produce a 'Context Analysis Report'; and (2) the Safety Property Analysis Agent (SPAAgent), which infers, instruments (via a SAFETY_PROPERTY_ASSERT macro), and iteratively validates safety-property assertions against a proof-of-concept (PoC) exploit through repeated compile/run cycles, producing a 'Property Analysis Report'. Both reports are concatenated with the original developer issue report into an 'enhanced issue report' that then drives a four-stage deterministic pipeline: report enhancement, vulnerability localization (file + code-element level), patch generation (T candidate patches via SEARCH/REPLACE), and PoC-validated majority-vote patch selection. The authors claim this is the first system to resolve vulnerability issues end-to-end directly from natural-language issue reports (rather than sanitizer logs or manually supplied fault locations/CWE labels), and the first hybrid agent+workflow paradigm applied to AVR.
threat_model: Not an adversarial/attacker threat model in the traditional security-paper sense; this is a defensive tooling paper. The 'threat' addressed is: real-world memory-safety and logic vulnerabilities (CWE-125 Out-of-Bounds Read, CWE-787 Out-of-Bounds Write, CWE-476 NULL Pointer Dereference, CWE-416 Use-After-Free, CWE-119, and others) already discovered by fuzzers/sanitizers in open-source C/C++ projects, which the system must autonomously patch given (a) a natural-language issue report, (b) the vulnerable repository, and (c) a working PoC that triggers a sanitizer error. Safety properties are used defensively as formal-ish invariants ('something bad never happens') whose violation corresponds to the vulnerability, not as an attacker capability model. Agent-side sandboxing is addressed as an operational-safety concern rather than an adversarial one: the Python Code Execution Toolkit runs LLM-generated code inside an isolated Docker container via llm-sandbox, with file-system and shell-command access replaced by stubs that raise exceptions; the paper reports seven observed attempts by the agent to perform forbidden operations (file-system access, file read, command execution), all of which were blocked by the sandbox.
limitations: Evaluated only on C/C++ open-source projects via a single benchmark family (SEC-bench Lite/Full), so generalization to other languages or to proprietary/closed-source codebases is untested. The paper itself flags that PoC-based patch selection can still choose a patch that avoids triggering the sanitizer yet breaks functionality: of 63 issues with at least one non-sanitizer-triggering candidate, 3 were not actually resolved due to functionality breaks, which the authors explicitly note as future work. Baselines for SWE-agent/OpenHands/Aider under GPT-4o and o3-mini on SEC-bench Full were unavailable (SEC-bench leaderboard did not report them), so RQ3's headline comparison against the 'best leaderboard system' is limited to the single OpenHands+Claude-3.7-Sonnet configuration rather than a full model-matched sweep. Resolution rate drops from 75.0% (Lite, 80 issues) to 67.5% (Full, 200 issues), which the authors attribute to increased issue difficulty in the larger set but do not further decompose. Cost and effectiveness depend on the chosen base LLM (DeepSeek-V3.2-Exp is explicitly the best-performing and cheapest of the three base models tried); results with weaker or non-frontier models are not explored. The sandbox-escape-attempt statistic (seven forbidden-operation attempts blocked) is reported only as an aggregate count with no further security analysis of what the agent tried or why, so it should not be over-read as a rigorous red-team evaluation of agent sandboxing.

datasets_used: SEC-bench Lite (80 issues, 13 C/C++ projects, subset of SEC-bench)
SEC-bench Full (200 issues, 29 C/C++ projects spanning 16 CWEs, e.g. CWE-125, CWE-787, CWE-476, CWE-416, CWE-119)
quantitative_results: SEC-bench Lite, patch selection ablation: PoC-based voting 75.0% vs. simple majority voting 62.5%
SEC-bench Lite, input-type ablation: issue-report input 75.0% vs. sanitizer-log input 63.8%
SEC-bench Lite, patch space size T=1: 49/80 resolved (61.3%); T=5: 60/80 ($0.0718); T=10: 61/80 ($0.0768)
SEC-bench Lite, base-model ablation (VulnResolverbase -> VulnResolverfull): GPT-4o 33.8%->51.3% (+51.9% rel.), o3-mini 35.0%->57.5% (+64.3% rel.), DeepSeek-V3.2-Exp 48.8%->75.0% (+53.8% rel.)
SEC-bench Full, best leaderboard baseline OpenHands + Claude 3.7 Sonnet: 34.0% resolved
SEC-bench Lite, VulnResolver best-in-project in 12/13 projects; SEC-bench Full best-in-project in 23/29 projects
Tool usage per issue (VulnResolverfull): ~34 total calls; CPCAgent 5.7 searches, 2.9 reads, 2.4 symbol resolutions; SPAAgent 6.2 PoC executions, 6.3 edits applied, 0.96 Python executions
Sandbox: 7 forbidden-operation attempts (file access, file read, command execution) observed and blocked by the Python execution sandbox
baselines_compared: SWE-agent (with Claude 3.7 Sonnet, GPT-4o, o3-mini, DeepSeek-V3.2-Exp)
OpenHands (with Claude 3.7 Sonnet, GPT-4o, o3-mini, DeepSeek-V3.2-Exp)
Aider (with Claude 3.7 Sonnet, GPT-4o, o3-mini, DeepSeek-V3.2-Exp)
SEC-bench Full leaderboard best system (OpenHands + Claude 3.7 Sonnet)
